name: Reflexive Verbs
summary: Reflexive verbs describe actions you do to yourself, using the reflexive pronouns.
explanation:
Reflexive verbs describe an action the subject does to itself: chiamarsi (to call oneself / to be named), svegliarsi (to wake up), lavarsi (to wash oneself). In the dictionary they end in -si.

To use them, conjugate the verb normally and add the matching reflexive pronoun before it: mi (myself), ti (yourself), si (himself/herself), ci (ourselves), vi (yourselves), si (themselves).

So chiamarsi becomes mi chiamo (I am called), ti chiami (you are called), si chiama (he/she is called). Many daily-routine verbs are reflexive in Italian.
pattern: mi / ti / si / ci / vi / si + verb

examples.en: They have a lot of fun.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the reflexive pronoun. Chiamo Luca means 'I call Luca', not 'My name is Luca'.
Putting the pronoun after the verb in normal sentences. It goes before: mi alzo, not alzo mi.
Using mio (my) for body parts. Italian says mi lavo le mani, with the reflexive plus the article.
Forgetting that si serves both lui/lei and loro.
Translating 'each other' literally; Italian reuses ci and si (ci vediamo).
tip: When you attach the verb to an infinitive or command, the pronoun joins the end: voglio alzarmi (I want to get up).
